Chapter   Methods and
7
Encapsulation
OCP exam objectives covered in this
chapter:
✓✓ Creating and Using Methods
■■   Create methods and constructors with arguments and
return values
■■   Create and invoke overloaded methods
■■   Apply the static keyword to methods and fields
✓✓ Applying Encapsulation
■■   Apply access modifiers
■■   Apply encapsulation principles to a class
examining them in detail. In this chapter, you’ll explore meth-
instance variables, access modifiers, and encapsulation.
Designing Methods
Every interesting Java program we’ve seen has had a main() method. You can write other
methods, too. For example, you can write a basic method to take a nap, as shown in
Figure 7.1.
F i g u r e 7.1        Method declaration
access modifier                 method name
optional specifier            parentheses (required)
```java
 1: public final void nap(int minutes) throws InterruptedException {
 2: // take a nap
 3: }
 4: method body
 5: This is called a method declaration, which specifies all the information needed to call
 6: the method. There are a lot of parts, and we’ll cover each one in more detail. Two of the
 7: parts—the method name and parameter list—are called the method signature.
 8: Table 7.1 is a brief reference to the elements of a method declaration. Don’t worry if it
 9: seems like a lot of information—by the time you finish this chapter, it will all fit together.
10: Ta b l e 7.1         Parts of a method declaration
11: Element                               Value in nap() example                            Required?
12: Access modifier                       public                                            No
13: Optional specifier                    final                                             No
14: Element                           Value in nap() example                         Required?
15: Return type                       void                                           Yes
16: Method name                       nap                                            Yes
17: Parameter list                    (int minutes)                                  Yes, but can be empty
18: Optional exception list           throws InterruptedException                    No
19: Method body*                      {                                              Yes, but can be empty
20:                           }
21: * Body omitted for abstract methods, which we will cover in the later in the book.
22: To call this method, just type its name, followed by a single int value in parentheses:
23: nap(10);
24: Let’s start by taking a look at each of these parts of a basic method.
25: Access Modifiers
26: Java offers four choices of access modifier:
27: private The private modifier means the method can be called only from within the same
28: class.
29: Default (Package-Private) Access With default access, the method can be called only from
30: classes in the same package. This one is tricky because there is no keyword for default
31: access. You simply omit the access modifier.
32: protected The protected modifier means the method can be called only from classes in
33: the same package or subclasses. You’ll learn about subclasses in Chapter 8, “Class Design.”
34: public The public modifier means the method can be called from any class.
35: in Chapter 4, “Making Decisions,” and you’ll see it again in the Chapter 9,
36: access control.
37: We’ll explore the impact of the various access modifiers later in this chapter. For now,
38: just master identifying valid syntax of methods. The exam creators like to trick you by
39: putting method elements in the wrong order or using incorrect values.
40: We’ll see practice examples as we go through each of the method elements in this sec-
41: tion. Make sure you understand why each of these is a valid or invalid method declaration.
42: Pay attention to the access modifiers as you figure out what is wrong with the ones that
43: don’t compile when inserted into a class:
44: public void walk1() {}
45: default void walk2() {} // DOES NOT COMPILE
46: void public walk3() {} // DOES NOT COMPILE
47: void walk4() {}
```
The walk1() method is a valid declaration with public access. The walk4() method is a
valid declaration with default access. The walk2() method doesn’t compile because default
is not a valid access modifier. The walk3() method doesn’t compile because the access modifier
is specified after the return type.
Optional Specifiers
There are a number of optional specifiers, but most of them aren’t on the exam. Optional spec-
ifiers come from the following list. Unlike with access modifiers, you can have multiple specifi-
ers in the same method (although not all combinations are legal). When this happens, you can
specify them in any order. And since these specifiers are optional, you are allowed to not have
any of them at all. This means you can have zero or more specifiers in a method declaration.
static The static modifier is used for class methods and will be covered later in this
chapter.
abstract The abstract modifier is used when a method body is not provided. It will be
covered in Chapter 9.
final The final modifier is used when a method is not allowed to be overridden by a sub-
class. It will also be covered in Chapter 8.
synchronized The synchronized modifier is used with multithreaded code. It is on the
1Z0-816 exam, but not the 1Z0-815 exam.
native The native modifier is used when interacting with code written in another lan-
guage such as C++. It is not on either OCP 11 exam.
strictfp The strictfp modifier is used for making floating-point calculations portable. It
is not on either OCP 11 exam.
Again, just focus on syntax for now. Do you see why these compile or don’t compile?
```java
1: public void walk1() {}
2: public final void walk2() {}
3: public static final void walk3() {}
4: public final static void walk4() {}
5: public modifier void walk5() {}              // DOES NOT COMPILE
6: public void final walk6() {}                 // DOES NOT COMPILE
7: final public void walk7() {}
```
The walk1() method is a valid declaration with no optional specifier. This is okay—it
is optional after all. The walk2() method is a valid declaration, with final as the optional
specifier. The walk3() and walk4() methods are valid declarations with both final and
static as optional specifiers. The order of these two keywords doesn’t matter. The walk5()
method doesn’t compile because modifier is not a valid optional specifier. The walk6()
method doesn’t compile because the optional specifier is after the return type.
The walk7() method does compile. Java allows the optional specifiers to appear before
the access modifier. This is a weird case and not one you need to know for the exam. We
are mentioning it so you don’t get confused when practicing.
Return Type
The next item in a method declaration is the return type. The return type might be an
actual Java type such as String or int. If there is no return type, the void keyword is used.
This special return type comes from the English language: void means without contents. In
Java, there is no type there.
Remember that a method must have a return type. If no value is returned,
the return type is void. You cannot omit the return type.
When checking return types, you also have to look inside the method body. Methods
with a return type other than void are required to have a return statement inside the
method body. This return statement must include the primitive or object to be returned.
Methods that have a return type of void are permitted to have a return statement with no
value returned or omit the return statement entirely.
Ready for some examples? Can you explain why these methods compile or don’t?
```java
 1: public void walk1() {}
 2: public void walk2() { return; }
 3: public String walk3() { return ""; }
 4: public String walk4() {}                       // DOES NOT COMPILE
 5: public walk5() {}                              // DOES NOT COMPILE
 6: public String int walk6() { }                  // DOES NOT COMPILE
 7: String walk7(int a) { if (a == 4) return ""; } // DOES NOT COMPILE
 8: Since the return type of the walk1() method is void, the return statement is optional.
 9: The walk2() method shows the optional return statement that correctly doesn’t return
10: anything. The walk3() method is a valid declaration with a String return type and a
11: return statement that returns a String. The walk4() method doesn’t compile because the
12: return statement is missing. The walk5() method doesn’t compile because the return type
13: is missing. The walk6() method doesn’t compile because it attempts to use two return
14: types. You get only one return type.
15: The walk7() method is a little tricky. There is a return statement, but it doesn’t always
16: get run. If a is 6, the return statement doesn’t get executed. Since the String always needs
17: to be returned, the compiler complains.
18: When returning a value, it needs to be assignable to the return type. Imagine there is a
19: local variable of that type to which it is assigned before being returned. Can you think of
20: how to add a line of code with a local variable in these two methods?
21: int integer() {
22: return 9;
23: }
24: int longMethod() {
25: return 9L; // DOES NOT COMPILE
26: }
27: It is a fairly mechanical exercise. You just add a line with a local variable. The type of
28: the local variable matches the return type of the method. Then you return that local vari-
29: able instead of the value directly:
30: int integerExpanded() {
31: int temp = 9;
32: return temp;
33: }
34: int longExpanded() {
35: int temp = 9L; // DOES NOT COMPILE
36: return temp;
37: }
```
This shows more clearly why you can’t return a long primitive in a method that returns
an int. You can’t stuff that long into an int variable, so you can’t return it directly either.
Method Name
Method names follow the same rules as we practiced with variable names in Chapter 2,
“Java Building Blocks.” To review, an identifier may only contain letters, numbers, $, or _.
Also, the first character is not allowed to be a number, and reserved words are not allowed.
Finally, the single underscore character is not allowed. By convention, methods begin with
a lowercase letter but are not required to. Since this is a review of Chapter 2, we can jump
right into practicing with some examples:
```java
1: public void walk1() {}
2: public void 2walk() {}       // DOES NOT COMPILE
3: public walk3 void() {}       // DOES NOT COMPILE
4: public void Walk_$() {}
5: public _() {}                // DOES NOT COMPILE
6: public void() {}             // DOES NOT COMPILE
```
The walk1() method is a valid declaration with a traditional name. The 2walk() method
doesn’t compile because identifiers are not allowed to begin with numbers. The walk3()
method doesn’t compile because the method name is before the return type. The Walk_$()
method is a valid declaration. While it certainly isn’t good practice to start a method name
with a capital letter and end with punctuation, it is legal. The _ method is not allowed since
it consists of a single underscore. The final line of code doesn’t compile because the method
name is missing.
Parameter List
Although the parameter list is required, it doesn’t have to contain any parameters. This
means you can just have an empty pair of parentheses after the method name, as follows:
```java
1: void nap(){}
```
If you do have multiple parameters, you separate them with a comma. There are a cou-
ple more rules for the parameter list that you’ll see when we cover varargs shortly. For now,
let’s practice looking at method declaration with “regular” parameters:
```java
1: public void walk1() {}
2: public void walk2 {}                      // DOES NOT COMPILE
3: public void walk3(int a) {}
4: public void walk4(int a; int b) {}        // DOES NOT COMPILE
5: public void walk5(int a, int b) {}
```
The walk1() method is a valid declaration without any parameters. The walk2()
method doesn’t compile because it is missing the parentheses around the parameter list.
The walk3() method is a valid declaration with one parameter. The walk4() method
doesn’t compile because the parameters are separated by a semicolon rather than a comma.
Semicolons are for separating statements, not for parameter lists. The walk5() method is a
valid declaration with two parameters.
Optional Exception List
In Java, code can indicate that something went wrong by throwing an exception. We’ll cover
this in Chapter 10, “Exceptions.” For now, you just need to know that it is optional and
where in the method declaration it goes if present. For example, InterruptedException
is a type of Exception. You can list as many types of exceptions as you want in this clause
separated by commas. Here’s an example:
```java
 1: public void zeroExceptions() {}
 2: public void oneException() throws IllegalArgumentException {}
 3: public void twoExceptions() throws
 4: IllegalArgumentException, InterruptedException {}
 5: You might be wondering what methods do with these exceptions. The calling method
 6: can throw the same exceptions or handle them. You’ll learn more about this in Chapter 10.
 7: Method Body
 8: The final part of a method declaration is the method body (except for abstract methods and
 9: interfaces, but you don’t need to know about either of those yet). A method body is simply
10: a code block. It has braces that contain zero or more Java statements. We’ve spent several
11: chapters looking at Java statements by now, so you should find it easy to figure out why
12: these compile or don’t:
13: public void walk1() {}
14: public void walk2()     // DOES NOT COMPILE
15: public void walk3(int a) { int name = 5; }
```
The walk1() method is a valid declaration with an empty method body. The walk2()
method doesn’t compile because it is missing the braces around the empty method body.
The walk3() method is a valid declaration with one statement in the method body.
You’ve made it through the basics of identifying correct and incorrect method declara-
tions. Now you can delve into more detail.
Working with Varargs
As you saw in Chapter 5, “Core Java APIs,” a method may use a varargs parameter (vari-
able argument) as if it is an array. It is a little different than an array, though. A varargs
parameter must be the last element in a method’s parameter list. This means you are
allowed to have only one varargs parameter per method.
Can you identify why each of these does or doesn’t compile? (Yes, there is a lot of prac-
tice in this chapter. You have to be really good at identifying valid and invalid methods for
the exam.)
```java
1: public void walk1(int... nums) {}
2: public void walk2(int start, int... nums) {}
3: public void walk3(int... nums, int start) {}    // DOES NOT COMPILE
4: public void walk4(int... start, int... nums) {} // DOES NOT COMPILE
```
The walk1() method is a valid declaration with one varargs parameter. The walk2()
method is a valid declaration with one int parameter and one varargs parameter. The
walk3() and walk4() methods do not compile because they have a varargs parameter in a
position that is not the last one.
When calling a method with a varargs parameter, you have a choice. You can pass in
an array, or you can list the elements of the array and let Java create it for you. You can
even omit the varargs values in the method call and Java will create an array of length
zero for you.
Finally! You get to do something other than identify whether method declarations are
valid. Instead, you get to look at method calls. Can you figure out why each method call
outputs what it does?
```java
  1: public static void walk(int start, int... nums) {
  2:    System.out.println(nums.length);
  3: }
  4: public static void main(String[] args) {
  5:    walk(1);                       // 0
  6:    walk(1, 2);                    // 1
  7:    walk(1, 2, 3);                 // 2
  8:    walk(1, new int[] {4, 5});     // 2
  9: }
 10: Line 19 passes 1 as start but nothing else. This means Java creates an array of length 0
 11: for nums. Line 20 passes 1 as start and one more value. Java converts this one value to an
 12: array of length 1. Line 21 passes 1 as start and two more values. Java converts these two
 13: values to an array of length 2. Line 22 passes 1 as start and an array of length 2 directly
 14: as nums.
 15: You’ve seen that Java will create an empty array if no parameters are passed for a
 16: vararg. However, it is still possible to pass null explicitly:
 17: walk(1, null);        // throws a NullPointerException in walk()
 18: Since null isn’t an int, Java treats it as an array reference that happens to be null. It
 19: just passes on the null array object to walk. Then the walk() method throws an exception
 20: because it tries to determine the length of null.
 21: Accessing a varargs parameter is just like accessing an array. It uses array indexing.
 22: Here’s an example:
 23: public static void run(int... nums) {
 24:    System.out.println(nums[1]);
 25: }
 26: public static void main(String[] args) {
 27:    run(11, 22);     // 22
 28: }
 29: Line 20 calls a varargs method with two parameters. When the method gets called, it
 30: sees an array of size 2. Since indexes are 0 based, 22 is printed.
 31: Applying Access Modifiers
 32: You already saw that there are four access modifiers: public, private, protected,
 33: and default access. We are going to discuss them in order from most restrictive to least
 34: restrictive:
 35: ■■    private: Only accessible within the same class
 36: ■■    Default (package-private) access: private plus other classes in the same package
 37: ■■    protected: Default access plus child classes
 38: ■■    public: protected plus classes in the other packages
 39: We will explore the impact of these four levels of access on members of a class. As you
 40: learned in Chapter 1, “Welcome to Java,” a member is an instance variable or instance
 41: method.
 42: Private Access
 43: Private access is easy. Only code in the same class can call private methods or access private
 44: fields.
 45: First, take a look at Figure 7.2. It shows the classes you’ll use to explore private and
 46: default access. The big boxes are the names of the packages. The smaller boxes inside them
 47: are the classes in each package. You can refer back to this figure if you want to quickly see
 48: how the classes relate.
 49: F i g u r e 7. 2   Classes used to show private and default access
 50: This is perfectly legal code because everything is one class:
 51: package pond.duck;
 52: public class FatherDuck {
 53:    private String noise = "quack";
 54:    private void quack() {
 55:        System.out.println(noise);          // private access is ok
 56:    }
 57:    private void makeNoise() {
 58:        quack();                            // private access is ok
 59:    } }
 60: So far, so good. FatherDuck makes a call to private method quack() on line 8 and uses
 61: private instance variable noise on line 5.
 62: Now we add another class:
 63: package pond.duck;
 64: public class BadDuckling {
 65:    public void makeNoise() {
 66:        FatherDuck duck = new FatherDuck();
 67:        duck.quack();                       // DOES NOT COMPILE
 68:        System.out.println(duck.noise);     // DOES NOT COMPILE
 69:    } }
 70: BadDuckling is trying to access an instance variable and a method it has no business
 71: touching. On line 5, it tries to access a private method in another class. On line 6, it tries
 72: to access a private instance variable in another class. Both generate compiler errors. Bad
 73: duckling!
 74: Our bad duckling is only a few days old and doesn’t know better yet. Luckily, you know
 75: that accessing private members of other classes is not allowed and you need to use a differ-
 76: ent type of access.
 77: Default (Package-Private) Access
 78: Luckily, MotherDuck is more accommodating about what her ducklings can do. She allows
 79: classes in the same package to access her members. When there is no access modifier, Java
 80: uses the default, which is package-private access. This means that the member is “private”
 81: to classes in the same package. In other words, only classes in the package may access it.
 82: package pond.duck;
 83: public class MotherDuck {
 84: String noise = "quack";
 85: void quack() {
 86: System.out.println(noise);           // default access is ok
 87: }
 88: private void makeNoise() {
 89:  quack();                          // default access is ok
 90: }
 91: }
 92: MotherDuck can refer to noise and call quack(). After all, members in the same class
 93: are certainly in the same package. The big difference is MotherDuck lets other classes
 94: in the same package access members (due to being package-private), whereas FatherDuck
 95: doesn’t (due to being private). GoodDuckling has a much better experience than
 96: BadDuckling:
 97: package pond.duck;
 98: public class GoodDuckling {
 99: public void makeNoise() {
100: MotherDuck duck = new MotherDuck();
101: duck.quack();                                    // default access
102: System.out.println(duck.noise);                  // default access
103: }
104: }
105: GoodDuckling succeeds in learning to quack() and make noise by copying its mother.
106: Notice that all the classes covered so far are in the same package pond.duck. This allows
107: default (package-private) access to work.
108: In this same pond, a swan just gave birth to a baby swan. A baby swan is called a cygnet.
109: The cygnet sees the ducklings learning to quack and decides to learn from MotherDuck
110: as well.
111: package pond.swan;
112: import pond.duck.MotherDuck;              // import another package
113: public class BadCygnet {
114: public void makeNoise() {
115: MotherDuck duck = new MotherDuck();
116: duck.quack();                       // DOES NOT COMPILE
117: System.out.println(duck.noise);     // DOES NOT COMPILE
118: }
119: }
120: Oh no! MotherDuck only allows lessons to other ducks by restricting access to the
121: pond.duck package. Poor little BadCygnet is in the pond.swan package, and the code
122: doesn’t compile.
123: Remember that when there is no access modifier on a member, only classes in the same
124: package can access the member.
125: Protected Access
126: Protected access allows everything that default (package-private) access allows and more.
127: The protected access modifier adds the ability to access members of a parent class. We’ll
128: cover creating subclasses in depth in Chapter 8. For now, we’ll cover the simplest possible
129: use of a child class.
130: Figure 7.3 shows the many classes we will create in this section. There are a number of
131: classes and packages, so don’t worry about keeping them all in your head. Just check back
132: with this figure as you go.
133: F i g u r e 7. 3   Classes used to show protected access
134: Bird
135: BirdWatcher
136: BirdWatcherFromAfar
137: First, create a Bird class and give protected access to its members:
138: package pond.shore;
139: public class Bird {
140: protected String text = "floating";                    // protected access
141: protected void floatInWater() {                        // protected access
142: System.out.println(text);
143: }
144: }
145: Next, we create a subclass:
146: package pond.goose;
147: import pond.shore.Bird;                    // in a different package
148: public class Gosling extends Bird {        // extends means create subclass
149: public void swim() {
150: floatInWater();                      // calling protected member
151: System.out.println(text);            // accessing protected member
152: }
153: }
```
This is a simple subclass. It extends the Bird class. Extending means creating a subclass
that has access to any protected or public members of the parent class. Running this
code prints floating twice: once from calling floatInWater(), and once from the print
statement in swim(). Since Gosling is a subclass of Bird, it can access these members even
though it is in a different package.
Remember that protected also gives us access to everything that default access does.
This means that a class in the same package as Bird can access its protected members.
```java
 1: package pond.shore;                            // same package as Bird
 2: public class BirdWatcher {
 3: public void watchBird() {
 4: Bird bird = new Bird();
 5: bird.floatInWater();                     // calling protected member
 6: System.out.println(bird.text);           // accessing protected member
 7: }
 8: }
 9: Since Bird and BirdWatcher are in the same package, BirdWatcher can access members
10: of the bird variable. The definition of protected allows access to subclasses and classes in
11: the same package. This example uses the same package part of that definition.
12: Now let’s try the same thing from a different package:
13: package pond.inland;
14: import pond.shore.Bird;                    // different package than Bird
15: public class BirdWatcherFromAfar {
16: public void watchBird() {
17: Bird bird = new Bird();
18: bird.floatInWater();                     // DOES NOT COMPILE
19: System.out.println(bird.text);           // DOES NOT COMPILE
20: }
21: }
22: BirdWatcherFromAfar is not in the same package as Bird, and it doesn’t inherit from
23: Bird. This means that it is not allowed to access protected members of Bird.
24: Got that? Subclasses and classes in the same package are the only ones allowed to access
25: protected members.
26: There is one gotcha for protected access. Consider this class:
27: package pond.swan;
28: import pond.shore.Bird;     // in different package than Bird
29: public class Swan extends Bird {      // but subclass of Bird
30:    public void swim() {
31:       floatInWater();              // subclass access to superclass
32:       System.out.println(text);    // subclass access to superclass
33:    }
34:    public void helpOtherSwanSwim() {
35:       Swan other = new Swan();
36:      other.floatInWater();        // subclass access to superclass
37:      System.out.println(other.text); // subclass access
38:                                        // to superclass
39:   }
40:   public void helpOtherBirdSwim() {
41:      Bird other = new Bird();
42:      other.floatInWater();                // DOES NOT COMPILE
43:      System.out.println(other.text);      // DOES NOT COMPILE
44:   }
45: }
46: Take a deep breath. This is interesting. Swan is not in the same package as Bird but does
47: extend it—which implies it has access to the protected members of Bird since it is a sub-
48: class. And it does. Lines 5 and 6 refer to protected members via inheriting them.
49: Lines 10 and 11 also successfully use protected members of Bird. This is allowed
50: because these lines refer to a Swan object. Swan inherits from Bird, so this is okay. It is sort
51: of a two-phase check. The Swan class is allowed to use protected members of Bird, and we
52: are referring to a Swan object. Granted, it is a Swan object created on line 9 rather than an
53: inherited one, but it is still a Swan object.
54: Lines 16 and 17 do not compile. Wait a minute. They are almost exactly the same as
55: lines 10 and 11! There’s one key difference. This time a Bird reference is used rather than
56: inheritance. It is created on line 15. Bird is in a different package, and this code isn’t inher-
57: iting from Bird, so it doesn’t get to use protected members. Say what now? We just got
58: through saying repeatedly that Swan inherits from Bird. And it does. However, the variable
59: reference isn’t a Swan. The code just happens to be in the Swan class.
60: It’s okay to be confused. This is arguably one of the most confusing points on the exam.
61: Looking at it a different way, the protected rules apply under two scenarios:
62: ■■    A member is used without referring to a variable. This is the case on lines 5 and 6. In
63: this case, we are taking advantage of inheritance and protected access is allowed.
64: ■■     A member is used through a variable. This is the case on lines 10, 11, 16, and 17. In
65: this case, the rules for the reference type of the variable are what matter. If it is a sub-
66: class, protected access is allowed. This works for references to the same class or a
67: subclass.
68: We’re going to try this again to make sure you understand what is going on. Can you
69: figure out why these examples don’t compile?
70: package pond.goose;
71: import pond.shore.Bird;
72: public class Goose extends Bird {
73: public void helpGooseSwim() {
74: Goose other = new Goose();
75: other.floatInWater();
76: System.out.println(other.text);
77: }
78: public void helpOtherGooseSwim() {
79: Bird other = new Goose();
80: other.floatInWater();           // DOES NOT COMPILE
81: System.out.println(other.text); // DOES NOT COMPILE
82: }
83: }
```
The first method is fine. In fact, it is equivalent to the Swan example. Goose extends
Bird. Since we are in the Goose subclass and referring to a Goose reference, it can access
protected members. The second method is a problem. Although the object happens to be
a Goose, it is stored in a Bird reference. We are not allowed to refer to members of the Bird
class since we are not in the same package and the reference type of other is not a subclass
of Goose.
What about this one?
```java
1: package pond.duck;
2: import pond.goose.Goose;
3: public class GooseWatcher {
4: public void watch() {
5: Goose goose = new Goose();
6: goose.floatInWater();      // DOES NOT COMPILE
7: }
8: }
```
This code doesn’t compile because we are not in the goose object. The floatInWater()
method is declared in Bird. GooseWatcher is not in the same package as Bird, nor does it
extend Bird. Goose extends Bird. That only lets Goose refer to floatInWater() and not
callers of Goose.
If this is still puzzling, try it. Type in the code and try to make it compile. Then reread
this section. Don’t worry—it wasn’t obvious to us the fi rst time either!
Public Access
Protected access was a tough concept. Luckily, the last type of access modifier is easy:
public means anyone can access the member from anywhere.
The Java module system redefines “anywhere,” and it becomes possible
to restrict access to public code. When given a code sample, you can
assume it isn’t in a module unless explicitly stated otherwise.
Let’s create a class that has public members:
```java
  1: package pond.duck;
  2: public class DuckTeacher {
  3: public String name = "helpful";            // public access
  4: public void swim() {                       // public access
  5: System.out.println("swim");
  6: }
  7: }
  8: DuckTeacher allows access to any class that wants it. Now we can try it:
  9: package pond.goose;
 10: import pond.duck.DuckTeacher;
 11: public class LostDuckling {
 12: public void swim() {
 13: DuckTeacher teacher = new DuckTeacher();
 14: teacher.swim();                                             // allowed
 15: System.out.println("Thanks" + teacher.name);                // allowed
 16: }
 17: }
 18: LostDuckling is able to refer to swim() and name on DuckTeacher because they are
 19: public. The story has a happy ending. LostDuckling has learned to swim and can fi nd its
 20: parents—all because DuckTeacher made members public.
 21: To review access modifiers, make sure you know why everything in Table 7.2 is true.
 22: Remember that a member is a method or field.
 23: Ta b l e 7. 2    Access modifiers
 24: A method in            can access                   Default (package-
 25: a        member                        private      private)          protected        public
 26: the same class                         Yes          Yes                 Yes            Yes
 27: another class in the same package      No           Yes                 Yes            Yes
 28: in a subclass in a different package   No           No                  Yes            Yes
 29: an unrelated class in a different      No           No                  No             Yes
 30: package
 31: Applying the static Keyword
 32: When the static keyword is applied to a variable, method, or class, it applies to the class
 33: rather than a specific instance of the class. In this section, you will see that the static key-
 34: word can also be applied to import statements.
 35: Designing static Methods and Fields
 36: Except for the main() method, we’ve been looking at instance methods. static methods
 37: don’t require an instance of the class. They are shared among all users of the class. You can
 38: think of a static variable as being a member of the single class object that exists indepen-
 39: dently of any instances of that class.
 40: You have seen one static method since Chapter 1. The main() method is a static
 41: method. That means you can call it using the class name:
 42: public class Koala {
 43: public static int count = 0;                        // static variable
 44: public static void main(String[] args) {            // static method
 45: System.out.println(count);
 46: }
 47: }
 48: Here the JVM basically calls Koala.main() to get the program started. You can do this
 49: too. We can have a KoalaTester that does nothing but call the main() method:
 50: public class KoalaTester {
 51: public static void main(String[] args) {
 52: Koala.main(new String[0]);          // call static method
 53: }
 54: }
 55: Quite a complicated way to print 0, isn’t it? When we run KoalaTester, it makes a call
 56: to the main() method of Koala, which prints the value of count. The purpose of all these
 57: examples is to show that main() can be called just like any other static method.
 58: In addition to main() methods, static methods have two main purposes:
 59: ■    For utility or helper methods that don’t require any object state. Since there is no need
 60: to access instance variables, having static methods eliminates the need for the caller
 61: to instantiate an object just to call the method.
 62: ■    For state that is shared by all instances of a class, like a counter. All instances must
 63: share the same state. Methods that merely use that state should be static as well.
 64: In the following sections, we will look at some examples covering other static concepts.
 65: Accessing a static Variable or Method
 66: Usually, accessing a static member like count is easy. You just put the class name before
 67: the method or variable and you are done. Here’s an example:
 68: System.out.println(Koala.count);
 69: Koala.main(new String[0]);
 70: Both of these are nice and easy. There is one rule that is trickier. You can use an instance
 71: of the object to call a static method. The compiler checks for the type of the reference and
 72: uses that instead of the object—which is sneaky of Java. This code is perfectly legal:
 73: Koala k = new Koala();
 74: System.out.println(k.count);                  // k is a Koala
 75: k = null;
 76: System.out.println(k.count);                  // k is still a Koala
 77: Believe it or not, this code outputs 0 twice. Line 6 sees that k is a Koala and count is a
 78: static variable, so it reads that static variable. Line 8 does the same thing. Java doesn’t
 79: care that k happens to be null. Since we are looking for a static, it doesn’t matter.
 80: Remember to look at the reference type for a variable when you see a
 81: static method or variable. The exam creators will try to trick you into
 82: thinking a NullPointerException is thrown because the variable happens
 83: to be null. Don’t be fooled!
 84: One more time because this is really important: what does the following output?
 85: Koala.count = 4;
 86: Koala koala1 = new Koala();
 87: Koala koala2 = new Koala();
 88: koala1.count = 6;
 89: koala2.count = 5;
 90: System.out.println(Koala.count);
 91: We hope you answered 5. There is only one count variable since it is static. It is set to
 92: 4, then 6, and finally winds up as 5. All the Koala variables are just distractions.
 93: Static vs. Instance
 94: There’s another way the exam creators will try to trick you regarding static and instance
 95: members. A static member cannot call an instance member without referencing an
 96: instance of the class. This shouldn’t be a surprise since static doesn’t require any instances
 97: of the class to even exist.
 98: The following is a common mistake for rookie programmers to make:
 99: public class Static {
100: private String name = "Static class";
101: public static void first() { }
102: public static void second() { }
103: public void third() { System.out.println(name); }
104: public static void main(String args[]) {
105: first();
106: second();
107: third();          // DOES NOT COMPILE
108: }
109: }
```
The compiler will give you an error about making a static reference to a nonstatic
method. If we fix this by adding static to third(), we create a new problem. Can you
figure out what it is?
All this does is move the problem. Now, third() is referring to nonstatic name. Adding
static to name as well would solve the problem. Another solution would have been to call
third as an instance method—for example, new Static().third();.
The exam creators like this topic. A static method or instance method can call a
static method because static methods don’t require an object to use. Only an instance
method can call another instance method on the same class without using a reference vari-
able, because instance methods do require an object. Similar logic applies for the instance
and static variables.
Suppose we have a Giraffe class:
```java
 1: public class Giraffe {
 2: public void eat(Giraffe g) {}
 3: public void drink() {};
 4: public static void allGiraffeGoHome(Giraffe g) {}
 5: public static void allGiraffeComeOut() {}
 6: }
 7: Make sure you understand Table 7.3 before continuing.
 8: Ta b l e 7. 3   Static vs. instance calls
 9: Type                                        Calling                                 Legal?
10: allGiraffeGoHome()                          allGiraffeComeOut()                     Yes
11: allGiraffeGoHome()                          drink()                                 No
12: allGiraffeGoHome()                          g.eat()                                 Yes
13: eat()                                       allGiraffeComeOut()                     Yes
14: eat()                                       drink()                                 Yes
15: eat()                                       g.eat()                                 Yes
16: Let’s try one more example so you have more practice at recognizing this scenario. Do
17: you understand why the following lines fail to compile?
18: public class Gorilla {
19:    public static int count;
20:    public static void addGorilla() { count++; }
21:    public void babyGorilla() { count++; }
22:    public void announceBabies() {
23:       addGorilla();
24:       babyGorilla();
25:    }
26:    public static void announceBabiesToEveryone() {
27:      addGorilla();
28:      babyGorilla();     // DOES NOT COMPILE
29:   }
30:   public int total;
31:   public static double average
32:      = total / count; // DOES NOT COMPILE
33: }
34: Lines 3 and 4 are fine because both static and instance methods can refer to a static
35: variable. Lines 5–8 are fine because an instance method can call a static method. Line 11
36: doesn’t compile because a static method cannot call an instance method. Similarly,
37: line 15 doesn’t compile because a static variable is trying to use an instance variable.
38: A common use for static variables is counting the number of instances:
39: public class Counter {
40: private static int count;
41: public Counter() { count++; }
42: public static void main(String[] args) {
43: Counter c1 = new Counter();
44: Counter c2 = new Counter();
45: Counter c3 = new Counter();
46: System.out.println(count);          // 3
47: }
48: }
49: Each time the constructor gets called, it increments count by 1. This example relies on
50: the fact that static (and instance) variables are automatically initialized to the default
51: value for that type, which is 0 for int. See Chapter 2 to review the default values.
52: Also notice that we didn’t write Counter.count. We could have. It isn’t necessary
53: because we are already in that class so the compiler can infer it.
54: Does Each Instance Have Its Own Copy of the Code?
55: Each object has a copy of the instance variables. There is only one copy of the code for
56: the instance methods. Each instance of the class can call it as many times as it would like.
57: However, each call of an instance method (or any method) gets space on the stack for
58: method parameters and local variables.
59: The same thing happens for static methods. There is one copy of the code. Parameters
60: and local variables go on the stack.
61: Just remember that only data gets its “own copy.” There is no need to duplicate copies of
62: the code itself.
63: static Variables
64: Some static variables are meant to change as the program runs. Counters are a common
65: example of this. We want the count to increase over time. Just as with instance variables,
66: you can initialize a static variable on the line it is declared:
67: public class Initializers {
68: private static int counter = 0;                  // initialization
69: }
70: Other static variables are meant to never change during the program. This type of
71: variable is known as a constant. It uses the final modifier to ensure the variable never
72: changes. Constants use the modifier static final and a different naming convention than
73: other variables. They use all uppercase letters with underscores between “words.” Here’s
74: an example:
75: public class Initializers {
76: private static final int NUM_BUCKETS = 45;
77: public static void main(String[] args) {
78: NUM_BUCKETS = 5; // DOES NOT COMPILE
79: }
80: }
```
The compiler will make sure that you do not accidentally try to update a final variable.
This can get interesting. Do you think the following compiles?
```java
 1: private static final ArrayList<String> values = new ArrayList<>();
 2: public static void main(String[] args) {
 3: values.add("changed");
 4: }
 5: It actually does compile since values is a reference variable. We are allowed to call
 6: methods on reference variables. All the compiler can do is check that we don’t try to reas-
 7: sign the final values to point to a different object.
 8: Static Initialization
 9: In Chapter 2, we covered instance initializers that looked like unnamed methods—just
10: code inside braces. Static initializers look similar. They add the static keyword to specify
11: they should be run when the class is first loaded. Here’s an example:
12: private static final int NUM_SECONDS_PER_MINUTE;
13: private static final int NUM_MINUTES_PER_HOUR;
14: private static final int NUM_SECONDS_PER_HOUR;
15: static {
16: NUM_SECONDS_PER_MINUTE = 60;
17: NUM_MINUTES_PER_HOUR = 60;
18: }
19: static {
20: NUM_SECONDS_PER_HOUR
21: = NUM_SECONDS_PER_MINUTE * NUM_MINUTES_PER_HOUR;
22: }
23: All static initializers run when the class is first used in the order they are defined. The
24: statements in them run and assign any static variables as needed. There is something
25: interesting about this example. We just got through saying that final variables aren’t
26: allowed to be reassigned. The key here is that the static initializer is the first assignment.
27: And since it occurs up front, it is okay.
28: Let’s try another example to make sure you understand the distinction:
29: private static int one;
30: private static final int two;
31: private static final int three = 3;
32: private static final int four;    // DOES NOT COMPILE
33: static {
34:    one = 1;
35:    two = 2;
36:    three = 3;                     // DOES NOT COMPILE
37:    two = 4;                       // DOES NOT COMPILE
38: }
39: Line 14 declares a static variable that is not final. It can be assigned as many times as
40: we like. Line 15 declares a final variable without initializing it. This means we can initial-
41: ize it exactly once in a static block. Line 22 doesn’t compile because this is the second
42: attempt. Line 16 declares a final variable and initializes it at the same time. We are not
43: allowed to assign it again, so line 21 doesn’t compile. Line 17 declares a final variable that
44: never gets initialized. The compiler gives a compiler error because it knows that the static
45: blocks are the only place the variable could possibly get initialized. Since the programmer
46: forgot, this is clearly an error.
47: Try to Avoid Static and Instance Initializers
48: Using static and instance initializers can make your code much harder to read. Every-
49: thing that could be done in an instance initializer could be done in a constructor instead.
50: Many people find the constructor approach is easier to read.
51: There is a common case to use a static initializer: when you need to initialize a static
52: field and the code to do so requires more than one line. This often occurs when you want
53: to initialize a collection like an ArrayList. When you do need to use a static initializer,
54: put all the static initialization in the same block. That way, the order is obvious.
55: Static Imports
56: In Chapter 1, you saw that we could import a specific class or all the classes in a package:
57: import java.util.ArrayList;
58: import java.util.*;
59: We could use this technique to import two classes:
60: import java.util.List;
61: import java.util.Arrays;
62: public class Imports {
63: public static void main(String[] args) {
64: List<String> list = Arrays.asList("one", "two");
65: }
66: }
67: Imports are convenient because you don’t need to specify where each class comes
68: from each time you use it. There is another type of import called a static import. Regular
69: imports are for importing classes. Static imports are for importing static members of
70: classes. Just like regular imports, you can use a wildcard or import a specific member. The
71: idea is that you shouldn’t have to specify where each static method or variable comes
72: from each time you use it. An example of when static imports shine is when you are refer-
73: ring to a lot of constants in another class.
74: In a large program, static imports can be overused. When importing from
75: too many places, it can be hard to remember where each static member
76: comes from.
77: The previous method has one static method call: Arrays.asList. Rewriting the code
78: to use a static import yields the following:
79: import java.util.List;
80: import static java.util.Arrays.asList;          // static import
81: public class StaticImports {
82: public static void main(String[] args) {
83: List<String> list = asList("one", "two"); // no Arrays.
84: }
85: }
```
In this example, we are specifically importing the asList method. This means that any
time we refer to asList in the class, it will call Arrays.asList().
An interesting case is what would happen if we created an asList method in our
StaticImports class. Java would give it preference over the imported one, and the method
we coded would be used.
The exam will try to trick you with misusing static imports. This example shows almost
everything you can do wrong. Can you figure out what is wrong with each one?
```java
 1: import static java.util.Arrays;        // DOES NOT COMPILE
 2: import static java.util.Arrays.asList;
 3: static import java.util.Arrays.*;      // DOES NOT COMPILE
 4: 
 5: public class BadStaticImports {
 6:    public static void main(String[] args) {
 7:        Arrays.asList("one");          // DOES NOT COMPILE
 8:    } }
 9: Line 1 tries to use a static import to import a class. Remember that static imports are
10: only for importing static members. Regular imports are for importing a class. Line
11: 3 tries to see whether you are paying attention to the order of keywords. The syntax is
12: import static and not vice versa. Line 6 is sneaky. The asList method is imported on
13: line 2. However, the Arrays class is not imported anywhere. This makes it okay to write
14: asList("one") but not Arrays.asList("one").
15: There’s only one more scenario with static imports. In Chapter 1, you learned that
16: importing two classes with the same name gives a compiler error. This is true of static
17: imports as well. The compiler will complain if you try to explicitly do a static import of
18: two methods with the same name or two static variables with the same name. Here’s an
19: example:
20: import static statics.A.TYPE;
21: import static statics.B.TYPE;          // DOES NOT COMPILE
22: Luckily, when this happens, we can just refer to the static members via their class name
23: in the code instead of trying to use a static import.
24: Passing Data among Methods
25: Java is a “pass-by-value” language. This means that a copy of the variable is made and the
26: method receives that copy. Assignments made in the method do not affect the caller. Let’s
27: look at an example:
28: public static void main(String[] args) {
29:    int num = 4;
30:    newNumber(num);
31:    System.out.println(num);     // 4
32: }
33: public static void newNumber(int num) {
34:    num = 8;
35: }
36: On line 3, num is assigned the value of 4. On line 4, we call a method. On line 8, the num
37: parameter in the method gets set to 8. Although this parameter has the same name as the
38: variable on line 3, this is a coincidence. The name could be anything. The exam will often
39: use the same name to try to confuse you. The variable on line 3 never changes because no
40: assignments are made to it.
41: Now that you’ve seen primitives, let’s try an example with a reference type. What do you
42: think is output by the following code?
43: public static void main(String[] args) {
44: String name = "Webby";
45: speak(name);
46: System.out.println(name);
47: }
48: public static void speak(String name) {
49: name = "Sparky";
50: }
```
The correct answer is Webby. Just as in the primitive example, the variable assignment is
only to the method parameter and doesn’t affect the caller.
Notice how we keep talking about variable assignments. This is because we can call
methods on the parameters. As an example, here is code that calls a method on the
StringBuilder passed into the method:
```java
1: public static void main(String[] args) {
2: StringBuilder name = new StringBuilder();
3: speak(name);
4: System.out.println(name); // Webby
5: }
6: public static void speak(StringBuilder s) {
7: s.append("Webby");
8: }
```
In this case, the output is Webby because the method merely calls a method on the
parameter. It doesn’t reassign name to a different object. In Figure 7.4, you can see how
pass-by-value is still used. The variable s is a copy of the variable name. Both point to the
same StringBuilder, which means that changes made to the StringBuilder are available
to both references.
F i g u r e 7. 4   Copying a reference with pass-by-value
StringBuilder
Pass-by-Value vs. Pass-by-Reference
Different languages handle parameters in different ways. Pass-by-value is used by many
languages, including Java. In this example, the swap method does not change the origi-
nal values. It only changes a and b within the method.
```java
 1: public static void main(String[] args) {
 2: int original1 = 1;
 3: int original2 = 2;
 4: swap(original1, original2);
 5: System.out.println(original1);    // 1
 6: System.out.println(original2);    // 2
 7: }
 8: public static void swap(int a, int b) {
 9: int temp = a;
10: a = b;
11: b = temp;
12: }
```
The other approach is pass-by-reference. It is used by default in a few languages, such as
Perl. We aren’t going to show you Perl code here because you are studying for the Java
exam and we don’t want to confuse you. The following example is in a made-up language
that shows pass-by-reference:
```java
 1: original1 = 1;
 2: original2 = 2;
 3: swapByReference(original1, original2);
 4: print(original1);    // 2 (not in Java)
 5: print(original2);    // 1 (not in Java)
 6: 
 7: swapByReference(a, b) {
 8: temp = a;
 9: a = b;
10: b = temp;
11: }
12: See the difference? In our made-up language, the caller is affected by variable assign-
13: ments made in the method.
14: To review, Java uses pass-by-value to get data into a method. Assigning a new primitive
15: or reference to a parameter doesn’t change the caller. Calling methods on a reference to an
16: object can affect the caller.
17: Getting data back from a method is easier. A copy is made of the primitive or reference
18: and returned from the method. Most of the time, this returned value is used. For example,
19: it might be stored in a variable. If the returned value is not used, the result is ignored.
20: Watch for this on the exam. Ignored returned values are tricky.
21: Let’s try an example. Pay attention to the return types.
22: public class ReturningValues {
23:    public static void main(String[] args) {
24:       int number = 1;                           // number=1
25:       String letters = "abc";                   // letters=abc
26:       number(number);                           // number=1
27:       letters = letters(letters);               // letters=abcd
28:       System.out.println(number + letters);     // 1abcd
29:    }
30:    public static int number(int number) {
31:      number++;
32:      return number;
33:   }
34:   public static String letters(String letters) {
35:      letters += "d";
36:      return letters;
37:   }
38: }
```
This is a tricky one because there is a lot to keep track of. When you see such questions
on the exam, write down the values of each variable. Lines 3 and 4 are straightforward
assignments. Line 5 calls a method. Line 10 increments the method parameter to 2 but
leaves the number variable in the main() method as 1. While line 11 returns the value, the
caller ignores it. The method call on line 6 doesn’t ignore the result, so letters becomes
"abcd". Remember that this is happening because of the returned value and not the method
parameter.
Overloading Methods
Now that you are familiar with the rules for declaring methods, it is time to look at cre-
ating methods with the same name in the same class. Method overloading occurs when
methods have the same name but different method signatures, which means they differ
by method parameters. (Overloading differs from overriding, which you’ll learn about in
Chapter 8.)
We’ve been showing how to call overloaded methods for a while. System.out.println
and StringBuilder’s append methods provide many overloaded versions, so you can pass
just about anything to them without having to think about it. In both of these examples,
the only change was the type of the parameter. Overloading also allows different numbers
of parameters.
Everything other than the method name can vary for overloading methods. This means
there can be different access modifiers, specifiers (like static), return types, and exception
lists.
These are all valid overloaded methods:
```java
 1: public void fly(int numMiles) {}
 2: public void fly(short numFeet) {}
 3: public boolean fly() { return false; }
 4: void fly(int numMiles, short numFeet) {}
 5: public void fly(short numFeet, int numMiles) throws Exception {}
 6: As you can see, we can overload by changing anything in the parameter list. We can
 7: have a different type, more types, or the same types in a different order. Also notice that
 8: the return type, access modifier, and exception list are irrelevant to overloading.
 9: Now let’s look at an example that is not valid overloading:
10: public void fly(int numMiles) {}
11: public int fly(int numMiles) {}           // DOES NOT COMPILE
```
This method doesn’t compile because it differs from the original only by return type.
The parameter lists are the same, so they are duplicate methods as far as Java is concerned.
What about these two? Why does the second not compile?
```java
 1: public void fly(int numMiles) {}
 2: public static void fly(int numMiles) {}            // DOES NOT COMPILE
 3: Again, the parameter list is the same. You cannot have methods where the only differ-
 4: ence is that one is an instance method and one is a static method.
 5: Calling overloaded methods is easy. You just write code and Java calls the right one. For
 6: example, look at these two methods:
 7: public void fly(int numMiles) {
 8: System.out.println("int");
 9: }
10: public void fly(short numFeet) {
11: System.out.println("short");
12: }
```
The call fly((short) 1) prints short. It looks for matching types and calls the appro-
priate method. Of course, it can be more complicated than this.
Now that you know the basics of overloading, let’s look at some more complex scenarios
that you may encounter on the exam.
Varargs
Which method do you think is called if we pass an int[]?
```java
 1: public void fly(int[] lengths) {}
 2: public void fly(int... lengths) {}           // DOES NOT COMPILE
 3: Trick question! Remember that Java treats varargs as if they were an array. This means
 4: that the method signature is the same for both methods. Since we are not allowed to over-
 5: load methods with the same parameter list, this code doesn’t compile. Even though the code
 6: doesn’t look the same, it compiles to the same parameter list.
 7: Now that we’ve just gotten through explaining that they are the same, it is time to men-
 8: tion how they are not the same. It shouldn’t be a surprise that you can call either method by
 9: passing an array:
10: fly(new int[] { 1, 2, 3 });
```
However, you can only call the varargs version with stand-alone parameters:
```java
1: fly(1, 2, 3);
2: Obviously, this means they don’t compile exactly the same. The parameter list is the
3: same, though, and that is what you need to know with respect to overloading for the exam.
4: Autoboxing
5: In Chapter 5, you saw how Java will convert a primitive int to an object Integer to add it
6: to an ArrayList through the wonders of autoboxing. This works for code you write too.
7: public void fly(Integer numMiles) {}
```
This means calling fly(3) will call the previous method as expected. However, what
happens if you have both a primitive and an integer version?
```java
 1: public void fly(int numMiles) {}
 2: public void fly(Integer numMiles) {}
 3: Java will match the int numMiles version. Java tries to use the most specific parameter
 4: list it can find. When the primitive int version isn’t present, it will autobox. However, when
 5: the primitive int version is provided, there is no reason for Java to do the extra work of
 6: autoboxing.
 7: Reference Types
 8: Given the rule about Java picking the most specific version of a method that it can, what do
 9: you think this code outputs?
10: public class ReferenceTypes {
11: public void fly(String s) {
12: System.out.print("string");
13: }
14: 
15: public void fly(Object o) {
16: System.out.print("object");
17: }
18: public static void main(String[] args) {
19: ReferenceTypes r = new ReferenceTypes();
20: r.fly("test");
21: System.out.print("-");
22: r.fly(56);
23: }
24: }
```
The answer is string-object. The first call is a String and finds a direct match. There’s
no reason to use the Object version when there is a nice String parameter list just waiting
to be called. The second call looks for an int parameter list. When it doesn’t find one, it
autoboxes to Integer. Since it still doesn’t find a match, it goes to the Object one.
Let’s try another one. What does this print?
```java
 1: public static void print(Iterable i) {
 2: System.out.print("I");
 3: }
 4: public static void print(CharSequence c) {
 5: System.out.print("C");
 6: }
 7: public static void print(Object o) {
 8: System.out.print("O");
 9: }
10: public static void main(String[] args){
11: print("abc");
12: print(new ArrayList<>());
13: print(LocalDate.of(2019, Month.JULY, 4));
14: }
```
The answer is CIO. The code is due for a promotion! The first call to print() passes
a String. As you learned in Chapter 5, String and StringBuilder implement the
CharSequence interface.
The second call to print() passes an ArrayList. Remember that you get to assume
unknown APIs do what they sound like. In this case, Iterable is an interface for classes
you can iterate over.
The final call to print() passes a LocalDate. This is another class you might not know,
but that’s okay. It clearly isn’t a sequence of characters or something to loop through. That
means the Object method signature is used.
Primitives
Primitives work in a way that’s similar to reference variables. Java tries to find the most
specific matching overloaded method. What do you think happens here?
```java
 1: public class Plane {
 2: public void fly(int i) {
 3: System.out.print("int");
 4: }
 5: public void fly(long l) {
 6: System.out.print("long");
 7: }
 8: public static void main(String[] args) {
 9: Plane p = new Plane();
10: p.fly(123);
11: System.out.print("-");
12: p.fly(123L);
13: }
14: }
```
The answer is int-long. The first call passes an int and sees an exact match. The sec-
ond call passes a long and also sees an exact match. If we comment out the overloaded
method with the int parameter list, the output becomes long-long. Java has no problem
calling a larger primitive. However, it will not do so unless a better match is not found.
Note that Java can only accept wider types. An int can be passed to a method taking a
long parameter. Java will not automatically convert to a narrower type. If you want to pass
a long to a method taking an int parameter, you have to add a cast to explicitly say nar-
rowing is okay.
Generics
You might be surprised to learn that these are not valid overloads:
```java
  1: public void walk(List<String> strings) {}
  2: public void walk(List<Integer> integers) {}            // DOES NOT COMPILE
  3: Java has a concept called type erasure where generics are used only at compile time.
  4: That means the compiled code looks like this:
  5: public void walk(List strings) {}
  6: public void walk(List integers) {}           // DOES NOT COMPILE
  7: We clearly can’t have two methods with the same method signature, so this doesn’t
  8: compile. Remember that method overloads must differ in at least one of the method
  9: parameters.
 10: Arrays
 11: Unlike the previous example, this code is just fine:
 12: public static void walk(int[] ints) {}
 13: public static void walk(Integer[] integers) {}
 14: Arrays have been around since the beginning of Java. They specify their actual types and
 15: don’t participate in type erasure.
 16: Putting It All Together
 17: So far, all the rules for when an overloaded method is called should be logical. Java calls
 18: the most specific method it can. When some of the types interact, the Java rules focus on
 19: backward compatibility. A long time ago, autoboxing and varargs didn’t exist. Since old
 20: code still needs to work, this means autoboxing and varargs come last when Java looks at
 21: overloaded methods. Ready for the official order? Table 7.4 lays it out for you.
 22: Ta b l e 7. 4    The order that Java uses to choose the right overloaded method
 23: Rule                                Example of what will be chosen for glide(1,2)
 24: Exact match by type                 String glide(int i, int j)
 25: Larger primitive type               String glide(long i, long j)
 26: Autoboxed type                      String glide(Integer i, Integer j)
 27: Varargs                             String glide(int... nums)
 28: Let’s give this a practice run using the rules in Table 7.4. What do you think this
 29: outputs?
 30: public class Glider2 {
 31: public static String glide(String s) {
 32: return "1";
 33: }
 34: public static String glide(String... s) {
 35: return "2";
 36: }
 37: public static String glide(Object o) {
 38: return "3";
 39: }
 40: public static String glide(String s, String t) {
 41: 
 42: return "4";
 43: }
 44: public static void main(String[] args) {
 45: System.out.print(glide("a"));
 46: System.out.print(glide("a", "b"));
 47: System.out.print(glide("a", "b", "c"));
 48: }
 49: }
 50: It prints out 142. The first call matches the signature taking a single String because
 51: that is the most specific match. The second call matches the signature, taking two String
 52: parameters since that is an exact match. It isn’t until the third call that the varargs version
 53: is used since there are no better matches.
 54: As accommodating as Java is with trying to find a match, it will do only one conversion:
 55: public class TooManyConversions {
 56: public static void play(Long l) {}
 57: public static void play(Long... l) {}
 58: public static void main(String[] args) {
 59: play(4);      // DOES NOT COMPILE
 60: play(4L);     // calls the Long version
 61: }
 62: }
 63: Here we have a problem. Java is happy to convert the int 4 to a long 4 or an
 64: Integer 4. It cannot handle converting to a long and then to a Long. If we had
 65: public static void play(Object o) {}, it would match because only one conversion
 66: would be necessary: from int to Integer. Remember, if a variable is not a primitive, it is
 67: an Object, as you’ll see in Chapter 8.
 68: Encapsulating Data
 69: In Chapter 2, you saw an example of a class with a field that wasn’t private:
 70: public class Swan {
 71: int numberEggs;         // instance variable
 72: }
 73: Why do we care? Since there is default (package-private) access, that means any class in
 74: the package can set numberEggs. We no longer have control of what gets set in your own
 75: class. A caller could even write this:
 76: mother.numberEggs = -1;
 77: This is clearly no good. We do not want the mother Swan to have a negative number
 78: of eggs!
 79: Encapsulation to the rescue. Encapsulation means only methods in the class with the
 80: variables can refer to the instance variables. Callers are required to use these methods. Let’s
 81: take a look at the newly encapsulated Swan class:
 82: public class Swan {
 83:    private int numberEggs;                     // private
 84:    public int getNumberEggs() {                // getter
 85:        return numberEggs;
 86:    }
 87:    public void setNumberEggs(int newNumber) { // setter
 88:        if (newNumber >= 0)                    // guard condition
 89:           numberEggs = newNumber;
 90:    } }
 91: Note that numberEggs is now private on line 2. This means only code within the class
 92: can read or write the value of numberEggs. Since we wrote the class, we know better than to
 93: set a negative number of eggs. We added a method on lines 3–5 to read the value, which is
 94: called an accessor method or a getter. We also added a method on lines 6–9 to update the
 95: value, which is called a mutator method or a setter. The setter has an if statement in this
 96: example to prevent setting the instance variable to an invalid value. This guard condition
 97: protects the instance variable.
 98: For encapsulation, remember that data (an instance variable) is private and getters/set-
 99: ters are public. Java defines a naming convention for getters and setters listed in Table 7.5.
100: Ta b l e 7. 5   Naming conventions for getters and setters
101: Rule                                         Example
102: Getter methods most frequently begin         public boolean isHappy() {
103: with is if the property is a boolean.          return happy;
104: Getter methods begin with get if the         public int getNumberEggs() {
105: property is not a boolean.                     return numberEggs;
106: Setter methods begin with set.               public void setHappy(boolean _happy) {
107:                                      }
```
In the last example in Table 7.5, you probably noticed that you can name the method
parameter to anything you want. Only the method name and property name have naming
conventions here.
It’s time for some practice. See whether you can figure out which lines follow these nam-
ing conventions:
```java
 1: private boolean playing;
 2: private String name;
 3: public boolean isPlaying() { return playing; }
 4: public String name() { return name; }
 5: public void updateName(String n) { name = n; }
 6: public void setName(String n) { name = n; }
 7: Lines 12 and 13 are good. They are private instance variables. Line 14 is correct. Since
 8: playing is a boolean, line 14 is a correct getter. Line 15 doesn’t follow the naming conven-
 9: tions because it should be called getName(). Line 16 does not follow the naming convention
10: for a setter, but line 17 does.
11: For data to be encapsulated, you don’t have to provide getters and setters. As long as
12: the instance variables are private, you are good. For example, this is a well-encapsulated
13: class:
14: public class Swan {
15: private int numEggs;
16: public void layEgg() {
17: numEggs++;
18: }
19: public void printEggCount() {
20: System.out.println(numEggs);
21: }
22: }
```
To review, you can tell it is a well-encapsulated class because the numEggs instance vari-
able is private. Only methods can retrieve and update the value.
Summary
As you learned in this chapter, Java methods start with an access modifier of public,
private, protected, or blank (default access). This is followed by an optional specifier such
as static, final, or abstract. Next comes the return type, which is void or a Java type.
The method name follows, using standard Java identifier rules. Zero or more parameters go
in parentheses as the parameter list. Next come any optional exception types. Finally, zero
or more statements go in braces to make up the method body.
Using the private keyword means the code is only available from within the same class.
Default (package-private) access means the code is available only from within the same pack-
age. Using the protected keyword means the code is available from the same package or sub-
classes. Using the public keyword means the code is available from anywhere. Both static
methods and static variables are shared by all instances of the class. When referenced from
outside the class, they are called using the classname—for example, StaticClass.method().
Instance members are allowed to call static members, but static members are not allowed
to call instance members. Static imports are used to import static members.
Java uses pass-by-value, which means that calls to methods create a copy of the param-
eters. Assigning new values to those parameters in the method doesn’t affect the caller’s
variables. Calling methods on objects that are method parameters changes the state of
those objects and is reflected in the caller.
Overloaded methods are methods with the same name but a different parameter list.
Java calls the most specific method it can find. Exact matches are preferred, followed by
wider primitives. After that comes autoboxing and finally varargs.
Encapsulation refers to preventing callers from changing the instance variables directly.
This is done by making instance variables private and getters/setters public.
Exam Essentials
Be able to identify correct and incorrect method declarations. A sample method declara-
tion is public static void method(String... args) throws Exception {}.
Identify when a method or field is accessible. Recognize when a method or field is
accessed when the access modifier (private, protected, public, or default access) does not
allow it.
Recognize valid and invalid uses of static imports. Static imports import static mem-
bers. They are written as import static, not static import. Make sure they are importing
static methods or variables rather than class names.
State the output of code involving methods. Identify when to call static rather than
instance methods based on whether the class name or object comes before the method.
Recognize that instance methods can call static methods and that static methods need
an instance of the object in order to call an instance method.
Recognize the correct overloaded method. Exact matches are used first, followed by
wider primitives, followed by autoboxing, followed by varargs. Assigning new values to
method parameters does not change the caller, but calling methods on them does.
Identify properly encapsulated classes.   Instance variables in encapsulated classes are
private. All code that retrieves the value or updates it uses methods. These methods are
allowed to be public.
