Chapter   Advanced Class
9
Design
OCP exam objectives covered in this
chapter:
✓✓ Reusing Implementations Through Inheritance
■■   Create and extend abstract classes
✓✓ Programming Abstractly Through Interfaces
■■   Create and implement interfaces
■■   Distinguish class inheritance from interface inheritance
including abstract classes
classes utilizing inheritance and polymorphism. In this chap-
with abstract classes. By creating abstract class definitions, you’re defining a platform that
other developers can extend and build on top of. We’ll then move on to interfaces and show
how to use them to design a standard set of methods across classes with varying implemen-
tations. Finally, we’ll conclude this chapter with a brief presentation of inner classes.
Creating Abstract Classes
We start our discussion of advanced class design with abstract classes. As you will see,
abstract classes have important uses in defining a framework that other developers can use.
Introducing Abstract Classes
In Chapter 8, you learned that a subclass can override an inherited method defined in a
parent class. Overriding a method potentially changes the behavior of a method in the par-
ent class. For example, take a look at the following Bird class and its Stork subclass:
class Bird {
```java
 1: public String getName() { return null; }
 2: public void printName() {
 3: System.out.print(getName());
 4: }
 5: }
 6: 
 7: public class Stork extends Bird {
 8: public String getName() { return "Stork!"; }
 9: public static void main(String[] args) {
10: new Stork().printName();
11: }
12: }
```
This program prints Stork! at runtime. Notice that the getName() method is overridden
in the subclass. Even though the implementation of printName() is defined in the Bird
class, the fact that getName() is overridden in the subclass means it is replaced everywhere,
even in the parent class.
Let’s take this one step further. Suppose you want to define a Bird class that other
developers can extend and use, but you want the developers to specify the particular type
of Bird. Also, rather than having the Bird version of getName() return null (or throw an
exception), you want to ensure every class that extends Bird is required to provide its own
overridden version of the getName() method.
Enter abstract classes. An abstract class is a class that cannot be instantiated and may
contain abstract methods. An abstract method is a method that does not define an imple-
mentation when it is declared. Both abstract classes and abstract methods are denoted with
the abstract modifier. Compare our previous implementation with this new one using an
abstract Bird class:
```java
 1: abstract class Bird {
 2: public abstract String getName();
 3: public void printName() {
 4: System.out.print(getName());
 5: }
 6: }
 7: 
 8: public class Stork extends Bird {
 9: public String getName() { return "Stork!"; }
10: public static void main(String[] args) {
11: new Stork().printName();
12: }
13: }
```
What’s different? First, the Bird class is marked abstract. Next, the getName() method
in Bird is also marked abstract. Finally, the implementation of getName(), including the
```java
1: braces ({}), have been replaced with a single semicolon (;).
```
What about the Stork class? It’s exactly the same as before. While it may look the same,
though, the rules around how the class must be implemented have changed. In particular,
the Stork class must now override the abstract getName() method. For example, the fol-
lowing implementation does not compile because Stork does not override the required
abstract getName() method:
```java
 1: public class Stork extends Bird {}       // DOES NOT COMPILE
 2: While these differences may seem small, imagine the Bird and Stork class are each writ-
 3: ten by different people. By one person marking getName() as abstract in the Bird class,
 4: they are sending a message to the other developer writing the Stork class: “Hey, to use this
 5: class, you need to write a getName() method!”
 6: An abstract class is most commonly used when you want another class to inherit proper-
 7: ties of a particular class, but you want the subclass to fill in some of the implementation
 8: details. In our example, the author of the Bird class wrote the printName() method but did
 9: not know what it was going to do at runtime, since the getName() implementation had yet
10: to be provided.
11: Override vs. Implement
12: Oftentimes, when an abstract method is overridden in a subclass, it is referred to as
13: implementing the method. It is described this way because the subclass is providing an
14: implementation for a method that does not yet have one. While we tend to use the terms
15: implement and override interchangeably for abstract methods, the term override is more
16: accurate.
17: When overriding an abstract method, all of the rules you learned about overriding meth-
18: ods in Chapter 8 are applicable. For example, you can override an abstract method with
19: a covariant return type. Likewise, you can declare new unchecked exceptions but not
20: checked exceptions in the overridden method. Furthermore, you can override an abstract
21: method in one class and then override it again in a subclass of that class.
22: The method override rules apply whether the abstract method is declared in an abstract
23: class or, as we shall see later in this chapter, an interface. We will continue to use override
24: and implement interchangeably in this chapter, as this is common in software develop-
25: ment. Just remember that providing an implementation for an abstract method is consid-
26: ered a method override and all of the associated rules for overriding methods apply.
27: Earlier, we said that an abstract class is one that cannot be instantiated. This means that
28: if you attempt to instantiate it, the compiler will report an exception, as in this example:
29: abstract class Alligator {
30: public static void main(String... food) {
31: var a = new Alligator(); // DOES NOT COMPILE
32: }
33: }
34: An abstract class can be initialized, but only as part of the instantiation of a nonabstract
35: subclass.
36: Defining Abstract Methods
37: As you saw in the previous example, an abstract class may include nonabstract methods, in
38: this case with the printName() method. In fact, an abstract class can include all of the same
39: members as a nonabstract class, including variables, static and instance methods, and inner
40: classes. As you will see in the next section, abstract classes can also include constructors.
41: One of the most important features of an abstract class is that it is not actually required
42: to include any abstract methods. For example, the following code compiles even though it
43: doesn’t define any abstract methods:
44: public abstract class Llama {
45: public void chew() {}
46: }
47: Although an abstract class doesn’t have to declare any abstract methods, an abstract
48: method can only be defi ned in an abstract class (or an interface, as you will see shortly). For
49: example, the following code won’t compile because the class is not marked abstract:
50: public class Egret { // DOES NOT COMPILE
51: public abstract void peck();
52: }
```
The exam creators like to include invalid class declarations like the Egret class, which
mixes nonabstract classes with abstract methods. If you see a class that contains an
abstract method, make sure the class is marked abstract.
Like the final modifier, the abstract modifier can be placed before or after the access
modifier in class and method declarations, as shown in this Tiger class:
```java
 1: abstract public class Tiger {
 2: abstract public int claw();
 3: }
 4: There are some restrictions on the placement of the abstract modifier. The abstract
 5: modifier cannot be placed after the class keyword in a class declaration, nor after the
 6: return type in a method declaration. The following Jackal and howl() declarations do not
 7: compile for these reasons:
 8: public class abstract Jackal {        // DOES NOT COMPILE
 9: public int abstract howl();        // DOES NOT COMPILE
10: }
11: It is not possible to define an abstract method that has a body, or default
12: implementation. You can still define a method with a body—you just can’t
13: mark it as abstract. As long as you do not mark the method as final, the
14: subclass has the option to override an inherited method.
15: Constructors in Abstract Classes
16: Even though abstract classes cannot be instantiated, they are still initialized through con-
17: structors by their subclasses. For example, does the following program compile?
18: abstract class Bear {
19: abstract CharSequence chew();
20: public Bear() {
21: System.out.println(chew());        // Does this compile?
22: }
23: }
24: 
25: public class Panda extends Bear {
26: String chew() { return "yummy!"; }
27: public static void main(String[] args) {
28:  new Panda();
29: }
30: }
31: Using the constructor rules you learned in Chapter 8, the compiler inserts a default no-
32: argument constructor into the Panda class, which first calls super() in the Bear class. The
33: Bear constructor is only called when the abstract class is being initialized through a sub-
34: class; therefore, there is an implementation of chew() at the time the constructor is called.
35: This code compiles and prints yummy! at runtime.
36: For the exam, remember that abstract classes are initialized with constructors in the
37: same way as nonabstract classes. For example, if an abstract class does not provide a con-
38: structor, the compiler will automatically insert a default no-argument constructor.
39: The primary difference between a constructor in an abstract class and a nonabstract
40: class is that a constructor in abstract class can be called only when it is being initialized by
41: a nonabstract subclass. This makes sense, as abstract classes cannot be instantiated.
42: Invalid Abstract Method Declarations
43: The exam writers are also fond of questions with methods marked as abstract for which
44: an implementation is also defined. For example, can you see why each of the following
45: methods does not compile?
46: public abstract class Turtle {
47: public abstract long eat()      // DOES NOT COMPILE
48: public abstract void swim() {}; // DOES NOT COMPILE
49: public abstract int getAge() { // DOES NOT COMPILE
50: return 10;
51: }
52: public void sleep;              // DOES NOT COMPILE
53: public void goInShell();        // DOES NOT COMPILE
54: }
```
The first method, eat(), does not compile because it is marked abstract but does not
end with as semicolon (;). The next two methods, swim() and getAge(), do not compile
because they are marked abstract, but they provide an implementation block enclosed
in braces ({}). For the exam, remember that an abstract method declaration must end in
a semicolon without any braces. The next method, sleep, does not compile because it is
missing parentheses, (), for method arguments. The last method, goInShell(), does not
compile because it is not marked abstract and therefore must provide a body enclosed in
braces.
Make sure you understand why each of the previous methods does not compile and that
you can spot errors like these on the exam. If you come across a question on the exam in
which a class or method is marked abstract, make sure the class is properly implemented
before attempting to solve the problem.
Invalid Modifiers
In Chapter 7, “Methods and Encapsulation,” you learned about various modifiers for meth-
ods and classes. In this section, we review the abstract modifier and which modifiers it is
not compatible with.
abstract and final Modifiers
What would happen if you marked a class or method both abstract and final? If you
mark something abstract, you are intending for someone else to extend or implement it.
But, if you mark something final, you are preventing anyone from extending or imple-
menting it. These concepts are in direct confl ict with each other.
Due to this incompatibility, Java does not permit a class or method to be marked both
abstract and final. For example, the following code snippet will not compile:
```java
1: public abstract final class Tortoise {        // DOES NOT COMPILE
2: public abstract final void walk();         // DOES NOT COMPILE
3: }
```
In this example, neither the class or method declarations will compile because they are
marked both abstract and final. The exam doesn’t tend to use final modifiers on classes
or methods often, so if you see them, make sure they aren’t used with the abstract modifier.
abstract and private Modifiers
A method cannot be marked as both abstract and private. This rule makes sense if you
think about it. How would you defi ne a subclass that implements a required method if the
method is not inherited by the subclass? The answer is you can’t, which is why the compiler
will complain if you try to do the following:
```java
1: public abstract class Whale {
2: private abstract void sing();       // DOES NOT COMPILE
3: }
4: 
5: public class HumpbackWhale extends Whale {
6: private void sing() {
7: System.out.println("Humpback whale is singing");
8: }
9: }
```
In this example, the abstract method sing() defi ned in the parent class Whale is not vis-
ible to the subclass HumpbackWhale. Even though HumpbackWhale does provide an implemen-
tation, it is not considered an override of the abstract method since the abstract method is
not inherited. The compiler recognizes this in the parent class and reports an error as soon
as private and abstract are applied to the same method.
While it is not possible to declare a method abstract and private, it is
possible (albeit redundant) to declare a method final and private.
If we changed the access modifier from private to protected in the parent class Whale,
would the code compile? Let’s take a look:
```java
1: public abstract class Whale {
2: protected abstract void sing();
3: }
4: 
5: public class HumpbackWhale extends Whale {
6: private void sing() { // DOES NOT COMPILE
7: System.out.println("Humpback whale is singing");
8: }
9: }
```
In this modified example, the code will still not compile, but for a completely differ-
ent reason. If you remember the rules for overriding a method, the subclass cannot reduce
the visibility of the parent method, sing(). Because the method is declared protected in
the parent class, it must be marked as protected or public in the child class. Even with
abstract methods, the rules for overriding methods must be followed.
abstract and static Modifiers
As you saw in Chapter 8, a static method cannot be overridden. It is defined as belonging
to the class, not an instance of the class. If a static method cannot be overridden, then
it follows that it also cannot be marked abstract since it can never be implemented. For
example, the following class does not compile:
```java
 1: abstract class Hippopotamus {
 2: abstract static void swim();      // DOES NOT COMPILE
 3: }
 4: For the exam, make sure you know which modifiers can and cannot be used with one
 5: another, especially for abstract classes and interfaces.
 6: Creating a Concrete Class
 7: An abstract class becomes usable when it is extended by a concrete subclass. A concrete
 8: class is a nonabstract class. The first concrete subclass that extends an abstract class is
 9: required to implement all inherited abstract methods. This includes implementing any
10: inherited abstract methods from inherited interfaces, as we will see later in this chapter.
11: When you see a concrete class extending an abstract class on the exam, check to make
12: sure that it implements all of the required abstract methods. Can you see why the following
13: Walrus class does not compile?
14: public abstract class Animal {
15: public abstract String getName();
16: }
17: 
18: public class Walrus extends Animal { // DOES NOT COMPILE
19: }
```
In this example, we see that Animal is marked as abstract and Walrus is not, making
Walrus a concrete subclass of Animal. Since Walrus is the first concrete subclass, it must
implement all inherited abstract methods—getName() in this example. Because it doesn’t,
the compiler reports an error with the declaration of Walrus.
We highlight the first concrete subclass for a reason. An abstract class can extend a non-
abstract class, and vice versa. Any time a concrete class is extending an abstract class, it
must implement all of the methods that are inherited as abstract. Let’s illustrate this with a
set of inherited classes:
```java
 1: abstract class Mammal {
 2: abstract void showHorn();
 3: abstract void eatLeaf();
 4: }
 5: 
 6: abstract class Rhino extends Mammal {
 7: void showHorn() {}
 8: }
 9: 
10: public class BlackRhino extends Rhino {
11: void eatLeaf() {}
12: }
```
In this example, the BlackRhino class is the first concrete subclass, while the Mammal and
Rhino classes are abstract. The BlackRhino class inherits the eatLeaf() method as abstract
and is therefore required to provide an implementation, which it does.
What about the showHorn() method? Since the parent class, Rhino, provides
an implementation of showHorn(), the method is inherited in the BlackRhino as a
nonabstract method. For this reason, the BlackRhino class is permitted but not required
to override the showHorn() method. The three classes in this example are correctly
defined and compile.
What if we changed the Rhino declaration to remove the abstract modifier?
class Rhino extends Mammal {       // DOES NOT COMPILE
```java
 1: void showHorn() {}
 2: }
 3: By changing Rhino to a concrete class, it becomes the first nonabstract class to extend
 4: the abstract Mammal class. Therefore, it must provide an implementation of both the
 5: showHorn() and eatLeaf() methods. Since it only provides one of these methods, the
 6: ­modified Rhino declaration does not compile.
 7: Let’s try one more example. The following concrete class Lion inherits two abstract
 8: methods, getName() and roar():
 9: public abstract class Animal {
10: abstract String getName();
11: 
12: }
13: 
14: public abstract class BigCat extends Animal {
15: protected abstract void roar();
16: }
17: 
18: public class Lion extends BigCat {
19: public String getName() {
20: return "Lion";
21: }
22: public void roar() {
23: System.out.println("The Lion lets out a loud ROAR!");
24: }
25: }
```
In this sample code, BigCat extends Animal but is marked as abstract; therefore, it is
not required to provide an implementation for the getName() method. The class Lion is
not marked as abstract, and as the first concrete subclass, it must implement all of the
inherited abstract methods not defined in a parent class. All three of these classes compile
successfully.
Reviewing Abstract Class Rules
For the exam, you should know the following rules about abstract classes and abstract
methods. While it may seem like a lot to remember, most of these rules are pretty
straightforward. For example, marking a class or method abstract and final makes it
unusable. Be sure you can spot contradictions such as these if you come across them on the
exam.
Abstract Class Definition Rules

questions.explanation: Concrete classes are, by definition, not abstract, so option A is incorrect. A concrete class must implement all inherited abstract methods, so option B is correct. The concrete class only needs to implement the inherited abstract methods. Finally, a method in a concrete class that implements an inherited abstract method overrides the method. While the method signature must match, the method declaration does not need to match, such as using a covariant return type or changing the throws declaration. For these reasons, option E is incorrect.

questions.explanation: An inner class can be marked abstract or final, just like a regular class, making option A correct. A top-level type, such as a class, interface, or enum, can only be marked public or default (package-private), making option B incorrect. Option C is incorrect, as a member inner class can be marked public, and this would not make it a top-level class. A .java file may contain multiple top-level classes, making option D incorrect. The precise rule is that there is at most one public top-level type, and that type is used in the filename. Finally, option E is correct. When a member inner class is marked private, it behaves like any other private members and can be referenced only in the class in which it is defined.
